stories.thread: Keonna Thomas's Charges and Travel Plans
stories.story: Keonna Thomas, 30, also known as "Young Lioness" and "Fatayat Al Khilafah," was charged by the FBI on Thursday with trying to travel overseas to fight for ISIS. An FBI complaint cites numerous social media messages from Thomas dating back to August 2013, including calls to join "brothers in the front lines" and references to a "mujahid's" death as a "wedding." The FBI said Thomas purchased an electronic visa to Turkey on March 23 and a ticket to Barcelona on March 26, with a March 29 departure and an April 15 return. Turkey is known as the easiest place to enter Syria and join ISIS, and an ISIS manual advises recruits to buy round-trip tickets to vacation spots like Spain. Thomas was charged with knowingly attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization and could be sentenced to 15 years in prison.
key_facts: Keonna Thomas, 30, charged by FBI on Thursday
Also known as "Young Lioness" and "Fatayat Al Khilafah"
Charged with trying to travel overseas to fight for ISIS
FBI complaint cites social media messages dating back to August 2013
Purchased electronic visa to Turkey on March 23
Purchased ticket to Barcelona on March 26 (March 29 departure, April 15 return)
Turkey known as easiest place to enter Syria and join ISIS
ISIS manual advises round-trip tickets to vacation spots like Spain
Charged with knowingly attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization
Could be sentenced to 15 years in prison
explicit_unknowns: It is not clear when or where she was arrested.

stories.story: Keonna Thomas is one of three women arrested this week on terror charges, with two New York women also taken into custody. On Thursday, Noelle Velentzas, 28, and her former roommate, Asia Siddiqui, 31, were arrested in New York. Federal prosecutors accused Velentzas and Siddiqui of planning to build an explosive device for attacks in the United States.
key_facts: Keonna Thomas is one of three women arrested this week on terror charges
Two New York women also taken into custody
Noelle Velentzas, 28, and Asia Siddiqui, 31, arrested in New York on Thursday
Accused of planning to build an explosive device for attacks in the United States

stories.thread: Broader Context of Terror Prosecutions and Threat Assessment
stories.story: In the past 18 months, the Justice Department's National Security Division has prosecuted or is prosecuting more than 30 cases of people attempting to travel abroad to join or provide support to terrorist groups. Of these cases, 18 allegedly involve support to ISIS. Homeland Security Secretary Jeh Johnson told reporters Thursday that the terrorist threat is "more decentralized, more diffuse, more complicated." He added that it "involves the potential lone wolf actor, it involves the effective use of social media, the Internet."
key_facts: In the past 18 months, Justice Department's National Security Division prosecuted or is prosecuting more than 30 cases
Cases involve people attempting to travel abroad to join or provide support to terrorist groups
18 of those cases allegedly involve support to ISIS
Homeland Security Secretary Jeh Johnson told reporters Thursday
Terrorist threat is "more decentralized, more diffuse, more complicated"
Threat involves "potential lone wolf actor" and "effective use of social media, the Internet"
